title: Ventricular Rhythms, AV Blocks & Conduction Defects Quiz

questions.text: The ventricles are described as the heart's:
Ventricular rhythms are recognized by which two ECG hallmarks?

answers.text: Upright P waves and narrow QRS complexes
Saw-tooth flutter waves and narrow QRS
Retrograde or missing P waves and wide, bizarre QRS > 0.12 s
Tall, peaked T waves and a normal QRS

questions.text: What is the typical QRS duration of a PVC?
PVCs that arise from the same anatomic site and look identical in the same lead are called:
A PVC that occurs between two normally conducted QRS complexes without disrupting the cardiac cycle is called:
Every third beat is a premature beat. This pattern is called:
What is the rate range for an idioventricular rhythm (IVR)?
Three or more ventricular escape beats in a row at 41–100 bpm is called:
Ventricular tachycardia is defined as:

answers.text: ≥ 3 PVCs in a row at a rate > 100 bpm

questions.text: Sustained VT is defined as VT that persists for more than:
VT in which the QRS complexes are all the same shape and amplitude is called:
Torsades de Pointes is characteristically associated with:
Coarse V-fib is distinguished from fine V-fib by:

answers.text: Wave amplitude — coarse ≥ 3 mm, fine < 3 mm
Heart rate above versus below 200 bpm
Presence of P waves in coarse V-fib

questions.text: Which of the following is a defining feature of V-fib?

answers.text: Chaotic, irregularly irregular deflections with no organized depolarization and no pulse
Inverted P waves before each QRS

questions.text: First-line treatment for stable but symptomatic V-Tach is:
First-line treatment for pulseless V-Tach and V-Fib is:
Asystole is defined by:

answers.text: Total absence of ventricular electrical activity, no pulse, no cardiac output
Inverted T waves with no P

questions.text: Treatment for asystole includes all of the following EXCEPT:
An atrial-only rhythm with no ventricular activity (atrial activity present, no QRS) is called:
First-degree AV block is best defined by:

answers.text: Progressive PR lengthening with a dropped beat
Some P waves not followed by a QRS, but PR is constant when conducted
PR interval prolonged (> 0.20 s) but constant; every P conducts to a QRS
Complete dissociation between P waves and QRSs

questions.text: Progressive PR-interval lengthening followed by a dropped QRS is the hallmark of:

answers.text: Second-degree AV block, Type I (Mobitz I / Wenckebach)
Second-degree AV block, Type II (Mobitz II)

questions.text: In Mobitz II second-degree AV block, the PR interval of conducted beats is:

answers.text: Progressively longer until a beat drops
Within normal range or slightly prolonged but constant
Variable with no relationship to QRS

questions.text: Mobitz II second-degree AV block reflects a conduction problem located:

answers.text: Below the AV node, within the His–Purkinje system

questions.text: In a 2:1 AV block, classification (Type I vs Type II) is based primarily on:

answers.text: QRS width — narrow ≤ 0.11 s suggests Type I, wide > 0.11 s suggests Type II

questions.text: Third-degree (complete) AV block is recognized by:

answers.text: Progressive PR lengthening with dropped beats
Atria and ventricles beating independently with no relationship between P waves and QRS complexes
A delta wave with a wide QRS

questions.text: Per the Block Jingle, which finding indicates a Wenckebach (Type I) block?

answers.text: If R is far from P
If some P's don't get through
If P's and Q's don't agree

questions.text: Per the Block Jingle, what indicates a third-degree AV block?

answers.text: If R is far from P
If some P's don't get through
If P's and Q's don't agree

questions.text: A complete bundle branch block is defined by a QRS duration of:
An incomplete bundle branch block is defined by a QRS duration of:
An rSR′ pattern ("rabbit ears") is the classic finding of:
Right bundle branch block (RBBB) on a 12-lead ECG typically shows:

answers.text: An rSR′ (M-shaped) pattern in V1 with a wide S in V6, often with right axis deviation
A W-shaped QRS in V1 and an M-shaped QRS in V6
Saw-tooth waves with a narrow QRS
A delta wave with a short PR interval

questions.text: Per the WiLLiaM MaRRoW shortcut, which morphology fits LBBB?

answers.text: M in V1, W in V6
W in V1, M in V6
Saw-tooth in both V1 and V6

questions.text: Left bundle branch block (LBBB) characteristically produces which pattern in V1 and V2?

answers.text: Negative QRS complexes with small R waves and deep S waves

questions.text: Pacing spikes appearing immediately before each QRS complex on a temporary paced rhythm represent:
Polymorphic VT with a twisting pattern around the baseline and a long QT interval is most likely:
